extracted_rules.resource: Right to hold priestly office
proof: [§6] There was in the days of Herod, the king of Judea, a certain priest named Zacharias, of the priestly division of Abijah. He had a wife of the daughters of Aaron, and her name was Elizabeth.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies Zacharias's status through his belonging to a specific priestly division (Abijah) and notes his wife's lineage as 'daughters of Aaron.' In Judean society of this period, priestly status was strictly hereditary through the male line, and marriage within priestly families was a normative expectation for maintaining the purity of the line.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule applies to a specific, small subset of the population defined by ancestral lineage (the descendants of Aaron/Levi).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to perform temple rituals
proof: [§9] Now it happened, while he executed the priest's office before God in the order of his division, [§10] according to the custom of the priest's office, his lot was to enter into the temple of the Lord and burn incense.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a formal system ('order of his division' and 'the custom') where priestly duties were allocated by lot and by specific time-restricted rotations. This indicates a legal/customary framework governing which individuals have access to the central religious resource (temple service) at specific times.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies only to the specific class of men who are already recognized as priests.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to name offspring
proof: [§60] It happened on the eighth day, that they came to circumcise the child; and they would have called him Zacharias, after the name of the father. [§61] His mother answered, 'Not so; but he will be called John.' [§62] They said to her, 'There is no one among your relatives who is called by this name.' [§63] They made signs to his father, what he would have him called.
extracted_rules.reasoning: When the mother attempts to name the child, the community (neighbors/relatives) challenges her and defers the final decision to the father. Even though the father is mute, he is treated as the final authority on the child's identity, demonstrating that the right to name a child is a patriarchal entitlement.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all men in the society who have fathered children.

extracted_rules.resource: Covenantal inclusion through circumcision
proof: [§60] It happened on the eighth day, that they came to circumcise the child; and they would have called him Zacharias, after the name of the father.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the circumcision of the male infant on the eighth day as a standard, expected communal event. In this society, circumcision was the concrete physical/legal mechanism for establishing a male's status within the group (Israel).
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all male children born into the ethnic/religious group.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to paternal name
proof: [§60] It happened on the eighth day, that they came to circumcise the child; and they would have called him Zacharias, after the name of the father. [§62] They said to her, 'There is no one among your relatives who is called by this name.'
extracted_rules.reasoning: The relatives' expectation that the child should be named after his father or a relative demonstrates a customary rule regarding the transmission of family identity through the male line.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to male children and their families during the naming process.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to remain unenrolled
proof: [§82] Now it happened in those days, that a decree went out from Caesar Augustus that all the world should be enrolled.
[§85] All went to enroll themselves, everyone to his own city.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a Roman imperial decree requiring every individual within the 'world' (the empire) to travel to their ancestral city for registration. This represents a loss of the freedom to avoid state surveillance and administrative control.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The decree is described as applying to 'all the world' and 'everyone,' indicating the broadest possible application within the society.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to bodily integrity
proof: [§103] When eight days were fulfilled for the circumcision of the child, his name was called Jesus, which was given by the angel before he was conceived in the womb.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text documents the practice of infant circumcision as a standard 'fulfillment' of religious law, which involves a permanent physical alteration to the male body at a specific age.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to all newborn males within the Jewish community, a significant but specific demographic.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to enter sacred spaces
proof: [§104] When the days of their purification according to the law of Moses were fulfilled, they brought him up to Jerusalem, to present him to the Lord
extracted_rules.reasoning: The mention of 'days of purification' being 'fulfilled' before visiting the temple implies that women were restricted from sacred spaces or formal religious presentation for a set period following childbirth.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies specifically to women following the biological event of childbirth.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom from religious consecration
proof: [§105] (as it is written in the law of the Lord, "Every male who opens the womb shall be called holy to the Lord")
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text cites a specific legal requirement where firstborn males are designated 'holy to the Lord,' a special status entailing specific presentation and sacrificial rituals.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies only to a specific subset of the population: the first male child born to a mother.

extracted_rules.resource: Autonomy of movement and action
proof: [§133] And he went down with them, and came to Nazareth. He was subject to them, and his mother kept all these sayings in her heart.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly states that as a child, Jesus was 'subject to' his parents, indicating a legal and customary lack of autonomy for minors in this society.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all children within the society until they reach the age of majority/adulthood.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to collect surplus revenue
proof: [§147] Tax collectors also came to be baptized, and they said to him, "Teacher, what must we do?" [§148] He said to them, "Collect no more than that which is appointed to you."
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a normative constraint on tax collectors, forbidding them from collecting more than the legally prescribed amount. This restricts their ability to extract personal profit through over-taxation, a common practice in Roman-era provincial administration.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule targets a specific professional class (publicani/tax collectors) within the Roman/Judean economic system.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to extra-legal seizure and extortion
proof: [§149] Soldiers also asked him, saying, "What about us? What must we do?" He said to them, "Extort from no one by violence, neither accuse anyone wrongfully. Be content with your wages."
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text mandates that soldiers refrain from using their physical power for extortion or false accusations. By commanding them to be 'content with your wages,' it explicitly removes their customary or de facto access to supplemental resources gained through the abuse of their position.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule applies specifically to the military class and their interactions with the civilian population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to read and teach in synagogues

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a broad category of males within the religious community, though limited by local recognition.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to local communal acceptance

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies only to a very small subset of the population (those claiming prophetic status) and only in their specific geography of origin.

extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the synagogue as a public space where the community ('all') gathers predictably on the Sabbath, indicating a customary right for members of the Jewish society to participate in these assemblies.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be declared ritually clean
proof: [§233] He charged him to "Tell no one, but go your way, and show yourself to the priest, and offer for your cleansing according to what Moses commanded, for a testimony to them."
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text references the Mosaic law requiring a person cured of leprosy to be examined and certified by a priest through specific offerings to be integrated back into the community.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to retain personal property

extracted_rules.resource: Right to social acceptance and reception
proof: [§198] He said, "Most assuredly I tell you, no prophet is acceptable in his hometown."

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to those identified as prophets within their local origins.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to participate in communal synagogue readings

extracted_rules.resource: Right to social association with religious elites

extracted_rules.reasoning: The objection raised by the Pharisees indicates a social and customary rule against shared meals (commensality) between 'righteous' observers of the law and tax collectors.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom of action

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all slaves/domestic servants in the society, who constitute a large portion of the population.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom of movement

extracted_rules.resource: Access to royal courts

extracted_rules.reasoning: The text confirms as an accepted common practice (and therefore a customary rule) that owners are permitted to perform the labor necessary to water their animals even on the Sabbath.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the category of agriculturalists and animal owners within the society.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to perform emergency labor
proof: [§679] He answered them, "Which of you, if your son or an ox fell into a well, wouldn't immediately pull him out on a Sabbath day?"
extracted_rules.reasoning: The rhetorical question points to a widely recognized social and legal exception where the prohibition of work on the Sabbath is waived in life-threatening emergencies involving kin or valuable property.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to all heads of households and parents in the community.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to preferential seating
proof: [§682] "When you are invited by anyone to a marriage feast, don't sit in the best seat, since perhaps someone more honorable than you might be invited by him, [§683] and he who invited both of you would come and tell you, 'Make room for this person.'"
extracted_rules.reasoning: The custom described treats seats at public feasts as a material resource allocated based on perceived 'honor' or social rank, enforced by the host.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This specifically concerns those attending formal elite social gatherings (marriage feasts).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be invited to feasts
proof: [§686] He also said to the one who had invited him, "When you make a dinner or a supper, don't call your friends, nor your brothers, nor your kinsmen, nor rich neighbors, or perhaps they might also return the favor, and pay you back. [§687] But when you make a feast, ask the poor, the maimed, the lame, or the blind;"
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author's exhortation to invite the poor specifically because they *cannot* repay the host reveals the standing rule of that society: that invitations were generally restricted to those with the wealth to reciprocate.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This reflects the general condition of the lower economic class in social life.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to claim share of paternal property
proof: [§722] The younger of them said to his father, 'Father, give me my share of your property.' He divided his livelihood between them.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a son's ability to request and receive his portion of the family estate during his father's lifetime, which the father complies with, indicating a recognized customary right to property division.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a significant subset of the population, namely sons within families with inheritable property.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from arbitrary dismissal
proof: [§745] He called him, and said to him, 'What is this that I hear about you? Give an accounting of your management, for you can no longer be manager.'
extracted_rules.reasoning: Upon a mere accusation of wasting possessions, the master is able to summarily dismiss the manager without a formal trial or secondary investigation, showing a lack of employment security for those in service roles.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to those in management/stewardship occupations.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to marry without legal/moral penalty
proof: [§743] Everyone who divorces his wife, and marries another, commits adultery. He who marries one who is divorced from a husband commits adultery.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly defines remarriage after divorce or marrying a divorced person as adultery, effectively stripping divorced individuals of the right to enter a new legitimate marriage without legal or social transgression.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to any person within the society who undergoes a divorce.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to retain property
proof: [§707] So therefore whoever of you who doesn't renounce all that he has, he can't be my disciple.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a strict rule for the group 'disciples': entering this status requires the total surrender of all material resources and goods.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies only to a very specific group of individuals choosing a particular religious/philosophical life path.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to food rations
proof: [§727] But when he came to himself he said, 'How many hired servants of my father's have bread enough to spare, and I'm dying with hunger!'
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text indicates that 'hired servants' (misthioi) as a class were entitled to bread as part of their employment, to the extent of having a surplus, contrasting them with the master's son in a state of destitution.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the broad category of free/hired laborers in the agricultural economy.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to legally remarry after divorce
proof: [§761] Everyone who divorces his wife, and marries another, commits adultery. He who marries one who is divorced from a husband commits adultery.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a legal/moral rule where a man who divorces and seeks a new marriage is categorized as an adulterer, effectively stripping the second marriage of its legitimacy and the man of his right to a new legal union.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to all adult men within the social/religious community who might seek divorce and remarriage.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to marry after divorce
proof: [§761] He who marries one who is divorced from a husband commits adultery.
extracted_rules.reasoning: By labeling any man who marries a divorced woman an adulterer, the law effectively prevents divorced women from entering into a new valid marriage, as the union is considered a crime.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to the subgroup of women who have been divorced from their husbands.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to personal rest after labor
proof: [§782] But who is there among you, having a servant plowing or keeping sheep, that will say, when he comes in from the field, 'Come immediately and sit down at the table,' [§783] and will not rather tell him, 'Prepare my supper, clothe yourself properly, and serve me, while I eat and drink. Afterward you shall eat and drink?'
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a standard social norm where a servant must complete domestic duties for the master after outdoor labor before they are permitted to see to their own sustenance or rest.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule characterizes the standard life and labor obligations for the entire class of servants/slaves in this society.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to recognition for labor
proof: [§784] Does he thank that servant because he did the things that were commanded? I think not.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage confirms a customary rule where a servant's labor, even when exhausting, does not entitle them to social or verbal recognition (thanks), as it is viewed strictly as an non-negotiable duty.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies broadly to the relationship between any master and servant in the socio-economic context described.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to social and religious reintegration
proof: [§787] As he entered into a certain village, ten men who were lepers met him, who stood at a distance. [§789] When he saw them, he said to them, 'Go and show yourselves to the priests.'
extracted_rules.reasoning: The rule requires those with skin diseases to 'stand at a distance' from the healthy and stipulates that only a priest can officially certify a recovery, which is a prerequisite for returning to society.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a specific rule targeting individuals with a particular medical condition (leprosy).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to retain or protect personal property
proof: [§806] In that day, he who will be on the housetop, and his goods in the house, let him not go down to take them away. Let him who is in the field likewise not turn back.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In the context of the 'Day of the Son of Man', the text establishes a prohibition on individuals attempting to secure or save their material goods, mandating the abandonment of property.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This command applies to anyone in the society who owns a house, goods, or works in a field.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to legal defense against adversaries
proof: [§815] A widow was in that city, and she often came to him, saying, 'Defend me from my adversary!'
[§817] yet because this widow bothers me, I will defend her, or else she will wear me out by her continual coming.'
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a widow's persistent demand for legal protection from a judge. Although the judge is described as unrighteous, his eventual decision to 'defend her' confirms that the legal system of the time recognized a widow's right to seek and receive judicial intervention against an adversary.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Widows represent a specific and recognizable subset of the female population with distinct legal needs in ancient society.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from unsworn or false accusations
proof: [§832] You know the commandments: 'Don't commit adultery,' 'Don't murder,' 'Don't steal,' 'Don't give false testimony,' 'Honor your father and your mother.'
extracted_rules.reasoning: The mention of the commandment 'Don't give false testimony' reflects a societal and legal rule against perjury, which serves as a protection for individuals within the legal system against false charges.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule is a general prohibition applying to almost everyone in the society.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to enter the Kingdom of God
proof: [§837] For it is easier for a camel to enter in through a needle's eye, than for a rich man to enter into the Kingdom of God.
extracted_rules.reasoning: While presented in a religious context, the text describes a systemic barrier to entry into the 'Kingdom'—which in this era carried significant political-communal weight—on the basis of inherited or accumulated wealth.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the segment of society defined by high wealth status.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to retain goods in case of wrongful exaction
proof: [§856] Zacchaeus stood and said to the Lord, 'Behold, Lord, half of my goods I give to the poor. If I have wrongfully exacted anything of anyone, I restore four times as much.'
extracted_rules.reasoning: Zacchaeus, a chief tax collector, references a four-fold restitution rule for wrongful exaction. This reflects a specific customary or legal penalty regarding property restoration for those in his profession who abuse their power.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies specifically to those in the occupation of tax collection who commit fraud.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to conduct trade in sacred public spaces
proof: [§856] He entered into the temple, and began to drive out those who bought and sold in it, saying to them, 'It is written, "My house is a house of prayer," but you have made it a "den of robbers"!'
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text records the enforcement of a rule prohibiting commercial transactions (buying and selling) within the Temple precincts, a central civic and religious site.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to all traders and customers specifically within the physical boundaries of the temple.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to refuse marriage to a deceased brother's widow
proof: [§933] They asked him, "Teacher, Moses wrote to us that if a man's brother dies having a wife, and he is childless, his brother should take the wife, and raise up children for his brother."
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text references the Mosaic law of levirate marriage, which imposes a legal and customary obligation on a man to marry his childless brother's widow to ensure the continuation of the brother's lineage.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies specifically to men who have brothers who die childless, a significant but specific subset of the population.

extracted_rules.resource: Retention of currency
proof: [§930] He said to them, "Then give to Caesar the things that are Caesar's, and to God the things that are God's."
extracted_rules.reasoning: In the context of the Roman occupation of Judea, the text affirms the legal requirement for the population to pay taxes to the Roman Emperor (Caesar).
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applied to nearly the entire adult population under Roman jurisdiction in the region.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to quadruple restitution
proof: [§864] Zacchaeus stood and said to the Lord, "Behold, Lord, half of my goods I give to the poor. If I have wrongfully exacted anything of anyone, I restore four times as much."
extracted_rules.reasoning: Zacchaeus, a tax collector, cites the legal/customary standard for restitution in cases of wrongful seizure of property, indicating that victims had a right to receive four times the value of what was taken.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to individuals who have been victims of financial misconduct or extortion.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to preferred seating and public honors
proof: [§951] "Beware of the scribes, who like to walk in long robes, and love greetings in the marketplaces, the best seats in the synagogues, and the best places at feasts;"
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the customary social privileges granted to the scribal class, including priority seating in religious and social gatherings and formal public recognition.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to the professional/religious class of scribes.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection of household property
proof: [§952] "who devour widows' houses, and for a pretense make long prayers: these will receive greater condemnation."
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text highlights a social reality where the property of widows ('houses') was vulnerable to exploitation or seizure by influential men, specifically scribes, using religious pretense.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the specific demographic of women whose husbands have died and who hold property.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to contribute to public religious funds
proof: [§953] He looked up, and saw the rich people who were putting their gifts into the treasury. He saw a certain poor widow casting in two lepta.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text demonstrates that a widow, despite her low economic and social status, had the legal and customary right to approach the temple treasury and make a financial contribution, just as the rich did.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Widows were a distinct and recognized population group within Judean society with specific socioeconomic characteristics.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from arbitrary arrest and imprisonment
proof: [§965] But before all these things, they will lay their hands on you and will persecute you, delivering you up to synagogues and prisons, bringing you before kings and governors for my name's sake.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes an impending societal shift where specific group members are subjected to summary arrest, religious trial (synagogues), and state imprisonment (kings and governors) based on their group identity.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule targets a specific ideological/religious sub-group rather than the general population.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from familial betrayal and execution
proof: [§969] You will be handed over even by parents, brothers, relatives, and friends. Some of you they will cause to be put to death.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text indicates that certain group members will lose the customary legal and social protections typically afforded by kinship, leading to their delivery to state authorities for execution.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific subset of the population defined by their allegiance to the teacher.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to enter the temple for instruction
proof: [§991] All the people came early in the morning to him in the temple to hear him.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text shows that the general public ('all the people') had the right to enter the temple precincts for the purpose of hearing public teaching, indicating high accessibility to central religious/political sites.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This describes a right held by the general population (the Jewish public) at large in Jerusalem.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to conduct judicial examinations
proof: [§1058] As soon as it was day, the assembly of the elders of the people was gathered together, both chief priests and scribes, and they led him away into their council, saying, [§1059] 'If you are the Christ, tell us.'
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes an established institutional body (the council of elders, priests, and scribes) with the recognized authority to seize individuals, bring them to a designated location, and conduct formal interrogations regarding religious and civil claims.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies specifically to a small elite class of religious and political leaders (priests and elders).

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from self-incrimination
proof: [§1063] They said, 'Why do we need any more witness? For we ourselves have heard from his own mouth!'
extracted_rules.reasoning: The council explicitly concludes that the defendant's own statements are sufficient for judgment, removing the necessity for external witnesses, which indicates a lack of protection against self-incrimination in this legal context.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While it describes a specific trial, it reflects the general procedural mindset of the contemporary judicial assembly regarding confession and witness requirements.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from corporal punishment before conviction
proof: [§1055] The men who held Jesus mocked him and beat him. [§1056] Having blindfolded him, they struck him on the face and asked him, 'Prophesy! Who is the one who struck you?'
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text illustrates that those held in custody by authorities were subject to physical violence and mockery by their guards prior to a final sentencing, indicating a lack of legal protection for the bodily integrity of detainees.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to any individual held in custody by the temple guards or council attendants.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to own and carry swords
proof: [§1028] Whoever has none, let him sell his cloak, and buy a sword. [...] [§1030] They said, 'Lord, behold, here are two swords.' He said to them, 'That is enough.'
extracted_rules.reasoning: The instruction to sell a basic necessity (a cloak) to purchase a weapon, and the subsequent possession of swords without legal interference from the immediate crowd or masters, reflects a society where the purchase and possession of personal weapons were permitted.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule regarding the ability to buy and carry a sword applies generally to the adult male population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to seasonal release from imprisonment
proof: [§1081] Now he had to release one prisoner to them at the feast.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a customary legal obligation of the Roman governor to release a prisoner during a specific religious festival, providing a mechanism for gaining freedom from incarceration.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to any individual currently in the subset of prisoners, which is a significant but specific population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to trial within local jurisdiction
proof: [§1070] But when Pilate heard Galilee mentioned, he asked if the man was a Galilean. [§1071] When he found out that he was in Herod's jurisdiction, he sent him to Herod, who was also in Jerusalem during those days.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a procedural rule where the place of origin or residency (jurisdiction) determines which magistrate has the authority to preside over a case.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to anyone under legal accusation within a province with internal jurisdictional borders.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from execution without capital crime
proof: [§1086] He said to them the third time, 'Why? What evil has this man done? I have found no capital crime in him. I will therefore chastise him and release him.'
extracted_rules.reasoning: Pilate, acting as judge, explicitly states the requirement of a 'capital crime' (a legal basis) for the sentence of death, attempting to follow procedural protection for the accused.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to any person brought before a Roman magistrate for trial.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to rest from labor
proof: [§1120] They returned, and prepared spices and ointments. On the Sabbath they rested according to the commandment.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The mention of resting 'according to the commandment' describes a societal rule in 1st-century Judea that mandated the cessation of work on the seventh day.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies broadly to the entire Jewish population within the province.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to participate in ruling council
proof: [§1114] Behold, a man named Joseph, who was a member of the council, a good and righteous man [§1115] (he had not consented to their counsel and deed), from Arimathaea, a city of the Jews
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies the existence of a 'council' (the Sanhedrin) where specific members (drawn from lineage/wealth/merit) have the power to deliberate and decide on legal/religious matters.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Membership in the council is restricted to a very small, elite group of men.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to have testimony believed
proof: [§1131] Now they were Mary Magdalene, Joanna, and Mary the mother of James. The other women with them told these things to the apostles. [§1132] These words seemed to them to be nonsense, and they didn't believe them.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In the cultural context of the first-century Mediterranean, women's testimony was often legally and socially dismissed as unreliable or 'nonsense'. The text depicts the male apostles immediately dismissing the women's report of the resurrection solely based on the identity of the messengers, requiring a male (Peter) to verify the facts before they are considered.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to all women within this social and legal context, which is a large demographic category.

extracted_rules.resource: Authority to validate testimony
proof: [§1132] These words seemed to them to be nonsense, and they didn't believe them. [§1133] But Peter got up and ran to the tomb. Stooping and looking in, he saw the strips of linen lying by themselves, and he departed to his home, wondering what had happened.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The narrative structure reinforces the rule that a man's physical observation or corroboration is the necessary standard for establishing the truth of a claim that was previously dismissed when coming from women. Peter's investigation serves as the formal validation step.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to men as a demographic category in terms of their social/legal standing as witnesses.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to religious message and community
proof: [§1168] and that repentance and remission of sins should be preached in his name to all the nations, beginning at Jerusalem.
extracted_rules.reasoning: This text explicitly removes a prior ethnic or national restriction (exclusivity to Israel) by mandating that the core ritual and social benefit of the group (remission of sins/preaching) be extended to 'all the nations'.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule is as broad as possible, explicitly encompassing all ethnic and national groups (all nations).
